summary: DeVault argues that FOSS projects should not use Discord for community communication, as it creates a walled garden that excludes the very users most passionate about free software. He contends that Discord's proprietary nature locks out users with accessibility needs, limited hardware, privacy concerns, or who live in sanctioned countries. The post frames the choice of communication platform as an investment decision — choosing Discord divests from the FOSS ecosystem while legitimizing proprietary platforms. DeVault extends the argument beyond Discord to all proprietary infrastructure including GitHub, Twitter, and YouTube, urging projects to choose FOSS alternatives like IRC, Matrix, SourceHut, and Codeberg. He calls for solidarity and mutual support within the FOSS ecosystem as essential to its long-term success.
key_points: Discord creates a two-tier community that makes free software advocates — a project's most passionate potential contributors — into second-class citizens
Choosing Discord excludes poor, disabled, privacy-conscious users and those in US-sanctioned countries, making it an actively exclusionary choice
Discord's interests fundamentally conflict with FOSS values: it seeks to domesticate users while FOSS treats them as peers and collaborators
Investing in proprietary platforms over FOSS alternatives is a short-term individualist play that undermines the ecosystem your own project depends on
The argument generalizes beyond Discord to all proprietary infrastructure — GitHub, Twitter, YouTube — and projects should critically examine every dependency on proprietary services
money_quotes: Using Discord partitions your community on either side of a walled garden, with one side that's willing to use the proprietary Discord client, and one side that isn't.
Choosing Discord is a choice that excludes poor and disabled users from your community.
Discord seeks to domesticate its users, where FOSS treats users as peers and collaborators. These ideologies are fundamentally in conflict with one another.
Choosing them is a short-term, individualist play which signals a lack of faith in and support for the long-term goals of the FOSS ecosystem as a whole.
Why should someone choose to use your FOSS project when you refused to choose theirs?
Solidarity and mutual support is the key to success.
If your project depends on proprietary infrastructure, I want you to have a serious discussion with your collaborators about why.
key_insight: FOSS projects that rely on proprietary communication platforms like Discord undermine their own values and exclude the very communities they should be serving, making platform choice an ethical and strategic decision, not merely a practical one.
